paragraphs.text: Heya! I am submitting this feedback to formally request that provisions be added to protect the ownership rights of digital assetsspecifically, video games. In recent times, various video game developers and distributors have openly suggested that players should "get used to not owning their games" and that "they will own nothing and be happy." Such sentiments are deeply concerning. As the industry increasingly shifts away from physical game sales, consumers who purchase digital titles are placed at greater risk of losing access to products they have paid full price for. A significant contributing factor is the rise of mandatory online connectivity, even for single-player experiences. This trend allows publishers to enforce strict controls over how a game can be used. For instance, features that are crucial for accessibilitysuch as customizable keybindings for one-handed playmay be excluded, with no ability for users to implement modifications due to these restrictions. This effectively limits access to the game for certain players and reduces the value of their purchase. Moreover, these always-online systems enable companies to revoke access to a game without notice, especially if it remains unplayed for an extended period. A user returning to a game after a few years might find it has been removed or made inaccessible, with no recourse or refund available. This undermines the basic principle of ownership. Another growing issue is the involvement of advocacy groups such as Collective Shout, which has recently campaigned to have certain titles removed from digital storefronts. They have urged payment processors like VISA and Mastercard to block purchases of gamesinitially those of an adult natureon platforms like Steam and Itch.io. While the stated intent is to protect children from explicit content, there are indications that unrelated games have also been targeted. This suggests a broader motive: controlling what adults are allowed to access and buy online. Such actions are deeply troubling and pose a threat to individual freedoms under the guise of moral safeguarding. In light of these developments, I urge you to consider adding legal protections for consumers' rights to digital propertyincluding video games. Safeguarding these rights ensures that companies cannot unilaterally revoke access to digital goods, nor can third-party organizations dictate what content individuals may legally purchase. When a consumer buys a digital product like a video game, even in the absence of a physical copy, they should be entitled to continued access. If companies claim we "do not own" our purchases, it raises serious questions about the legitimacy of revoking access after payment has been made. In such cases, one could reasonably argue that consumers should be permitted to access those products through unofficial means if access is denied. Thank you for your time and consideration. I hope you will take this matter seriously and work to ensure that digital ownership rights are clearly protected under the law.
